| First game          = {{flagdeco|FRA}} '''Ubangi-Shari''' 5–1 [[Cameroon national football team|French Cameroon]] {{flagdeco|FRA}} <br /> ([[Ubangi-Shari]]; unknown date 1956)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablesc/cafr-intres.html |title=Central African Republic – List of International Matches |author=Barrie Courtney |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=2013-04-03 |archive-date=4 February 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230204140636/https://www.rsssf.org/tablesc/cafr-intres.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
| Largest win         = {{fb|CTA}} 6–0 {{fb-rt|BHU}}<br> ([[Colombo]], [[Sri Lanka]]; 22 March 2024)
| Largest loss        = {{nowrap|{{fb|CIV}} 11–0 {{fb-rt|CTA}} <br /> ([[Abidjan]], [[Ivory Coast]]; 27 December 1961)}}
| Regional name       = [[African Nations Championship]]
| Regional cup apps   = 1
| Regional cup first  = [[2024 African Nations Championship|2024]]
| Regional cup best   = Group stage ([[2024 African Nations Championship|2024]])
}}

The '''Central African Republic national football team''' (French: ''Équipe de République centrafricaine de football''), nicknamed ''Les Fauves'', is the national team of the [[Central African Republic]] and is controlled by the [[Central African Football Federation]]. They are a member of [[Confederation of African Football|CAF]]. Despite being traditionally one of the weakest teams in Africa and the world, they have had some success. They won the [[2009 CEMAC Cup]] by beating [[Gabon national football team|Gabon]] in the semi-finals and [[Equatorial Guinea national football team|Equatorial Guinea]] in the final 3–0. Their FIFA ranking rose from 202nd in August 2010 to 89th by July 2011. On 10 October 2010, they earned a shock [[2012 Africa Cup of Nations qualification|2012 Africa Cup of Nations qualifier]] win at home against [[Algeria national football team|Algeria]] 2–0, which put them top of their qualification group. The team won its first FIFA World Cup qualifier on 2 June 2012 after beating [[Botswana national football team|Botswana]] 2–0 at home.<ref name=elim>{{cite web | access-date=2019-09-02 | url=http://centrafriquefootball.cf/eliminatoires-can-2019-la-liste-des-23-fauves-pour-affronter-la-guinee-2/ | title=Éliminatoires CAN 2019: La liste des 23 fauves pour affronter la Guinée | language=fr-FR | website=Centrafrique Football | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190726195133/http://centrafriquefootball.cf/eliminatoires-can-2019-la-liste-des-23-fauves-pour-affronter-la-guinee-2/ | archive-date=2019-07-26 | url-status=dead }}</ref><ref name=car>{{cite web|url=https://www.national-football-teams.com/country/39/2019/Central_African_Republic.html|title=CAR national football team 2018|website=national-football-teams.com|publisher=National Football Teams|access-date=25 February 2019|archive-date=10 May 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190510120605/https://www.national-football-teams.com/country/39/2019/Central_African_Republic.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

==History==
===Early years (1956–1989)===
[[File:Central African Republic National Football Team in 1972.jpg|thumb|Central African Republic Team in 1972]]
The team made its debut under the name of [[Ubangi-Shari]] in 1956 against [[Cameroon national football team|French Cameroon]]. The [[Central African Football Federation]] was founded in 1961 and joined [[FIFA]] in 1964 and [[Confederation of African Football|CAF]] in 1965.

Their first competitive match was in the 1961 Friendship Games in [[Ivory Coast]] where Central African Republic drew twice against [[Upper Volta national football team|Upper Volta]] and [[Liberia national football team|Liberia]] before losing to the hosts and failing to progress to the next round.

Central African Republic entered the [[1974 African Cup of Nations qualification]] for the first time, progressing due to [[Gabon national football team|Gabon]]'s withdrawal but were then eliminated 5–4 on aggregate by [[Ivory Coast national football team|Ivory Coast]], a tie which saw the second leg abandoned at half-time after arguments between players of both sides, forcing a replay in [[Lagos]] which Ivory Coast won 5–1.

They took part in [[UDEAC Cup#1984 UDEAC Cup|1984 UDEAC Cup]] where they qualified to the knock-out rounds on goal-difference over [[Equatorial Guinea national football team|Equatorial Guinea]] but were then heavily beaten by [[Cameroon national football team|Cameroon]] 7–1 before beating [[Gabon national football team|Gabon]] on penalties to finish third. However, in the [[UDEAC Cup#1988 UDEAC Cup|1988 UDEAC Cup]], Gabon would get their revenge, beating Central African Republic in the semi-finals. The following year Central African Republic would host the [[UDEAC Cup#1989 UDEAC Cup|1989 UDEAC Cup]], making it to the finals, beating Gabon on the way, before losing 2–1 to [[Cameroon national football team|Cameroon]].

===Sporadic matches (1990–2008)===
During the 1990s, Central African Republic played very few international games, withdrawing from the [[1996 African Cup of Nations qualification]] without playing a match. They returned to competition in the [[UNIFAC Cup]] in 1999, winning three times and losing twice to finish second.

Central African Republic participated in the [[2002 FIFA World Cup qualification – CAF first round|2002 World Cup qualifiers]] for the first time, losing in the first round to [[Zimbabwe national football team|Zimbabwe]]. They reached the final of the [[2003 CEMAC Cup|inaugural CEMAC Cup]], losing to an amateur [[Cameroon national football team|Cameroon]] team that they'd drawn with a week earlier in the tournament.

===Promising victories (2009–2019)===
In 2009, Central African Republic hosted the [[2009 CEMAC Cup]] where they defeated [[Equatorial Guinea national football team|Equatorial Guinea]] 3–0 in the final, with goals from [[Salif Kéïta (Central African footballer)|Salif Kéïta]] and a brace from [[Hilaire Momi]] to claim their first trophy. 

During the [[2012 Africa Cup of Nations qualification|2012 Africa Cup of Nations qualifiers]], Central African Republic achieved a historic 2–0 home victory over the top seeded [[Algeria national football team|Algeria]], a team that had recently competed at the [[2010 FIFA World Cup]]. In July 2011, they climbed to 89th place in the FIFA world rankings, having occupied 202nd place as recently as August 2010. 

On 2 June 2012, they obtained their first victory in [[2014 FIFA World Cup qualification (CAF)|World Cup qualification]], beating [[Botswana national football team|Botswana]] 2–0 at home. Despite that positive result, Central African Republic finished bottom of their group. On 15 June 2012, despite being reduced to ten men, they achieved another impressive victory, beating [[Egypt national football team|Egypt]] 3–2 in the [[2013 Africa Cup of Nations qualification|2013 Africa Cup of Nations qualifiers]], inflicting the first home defeat on the Egyptians in AFCON qualifiers since 1965. Thanks to a 1–1 draw at home in the second leg, Central African Republic eliminated the Egyptians, but in the second round they were defeated by [[Burkina Faso national football team|Burkina Faso]].

===Missed opportunities (2020–present)===

[[File:Hugo Gambor RC Lens - USL Dunkerque (08-07-2023) 22.jpg|thumb|150px|right|[[Hugo Gambor]] played his first game for the Central African Republic in 2024]]

On 30 March 2021, in the last group stage match of the [[2021 Africa Cup of Nations qualification|Africa Cup of Nations qualification]], Central African Republic lost 1–0 at home against [[Mauritania national football team|Mauritania]] to finish bottom of their group; hence, they missed the opportunity, had they won, to finish second which would have qualified them for the final tournament for the first time in their history.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.cafonline.com/total-africa-cup-of-nations/2021/matches/2032542/ |title=Central African Republic 0–1 Mauritania |publisher=CAF |date=30 March 2021 }}</ref>

On 17 June 2023, Central African Republic needed a win at home against [[Angola national football team|Angola]] on matchday 5 of the [[2023 Africa Cup of Nations qualification|qualifications]] to secure their first ever participation in the Africa Cup of Nations;<ref>{{cite web |url=https://sportnewsafrica.com/en/at-a-glance/afcon-2023-q-central-african-republic-one-step-away-from-glory/ |title=AFCON 2023 (Q): Central African Republic one step away from glory |publisher=Sport News Africa |date=17 June 2023 |access-date=17 June 2023 |archive-date=17 June 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230617154247/https://sportnewsafrica.com/en/at-a-glance/afcon-2023-q-central-african-republic-one-step-away-from-glory/ |url-status=live }}</ref> however, the match ended in a 2–1 defeat and a drop to third place.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.vavel.com/en-us/soccer/2023/06/17/1149325-central-african-republic-vs-angola-live-stream-how-to-watch-and-score-updates-in-africa-cup-of-nations.html |title=Goals and Highlights: Central African Republic 1-2 Angola in Africa Cup Of Nations Qualifiers |publisher=vavel.com |date=17 June 2023 |access-date=17 June 2023 |archive-date=17 June 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230617154245/https://www.vavel.com/en-us/soccer/2023/06/17/1149325-central-african-republic-vs-angola-live-stream-how-to-watch-and-score-updates-in-africa-cup-of-nations.html |url-status=live }}</ref> In the final group stage match, the national team lost 2–1 to [[Ghana national football team|Ghana]], despite a 1–0 lead, which ended their last hope of qualification.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.bbc.com/pidgin/live/sport-66744144 |title=Kudus, Nuamah goals secure Afcon 2023 place for Ghana |publisher=BBC Sport |date=7 September 2023 }}</ref>

==== FIFA Series tournament ====
In 2024, Central African Republic played their first ever tournament outside Africa where [[FIFA]] invited them to the [[2024 FIFA Series]] matches on from 22 to 25 March 2024 held in [[Colombo]] in Sri Lanka where they would face [[Bhutan national football team|Bhutan]] and [[Papua New Guinea national soccer team|Papua New Guinea]].

==Results and fixtures==
The following is a list of match results in the last 12 months, as well as any future matches that have been scheduled.

== Coaching history ==
:Caretaker managers are listed in ''italics''.
{{div col|colwidth=25em}}
*{{flagicon|URS}} [[Evgeni Rogov]] (1973–1976)
*{{flagicon|CTA}} [[Étienne Momokoamas]] (2004–2006)
*{{flagicon|CTA}} [[Francois Yanguere]] (2006–2008)
*{{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Jules Accorsi]] (2010–2012)
*{{flagicon|CTA}} ''[[Herve Lougoundji]]'' (2012–2014)
*{{flagicon|SUI}} [[Raoul Savoy]] (2014–2015)
*{{flagicon|FRA|1974}} [[Blaise Kopogo]] (2015)
*{{flagicon|CTA}} [[Herve Lougoundji]] (2015–2017)
*{{flagicon|SUI}} [[Raoul Savoy]] (2017–2019)
*{{flagicon|CIV}} [[François Zahoui]] (2019–2021)
*{{flagicon|SUI}} [[Raoul Savoy]] (2021–2024)
*{{flagicon|CTA}} ''[[Eloge Enza Yamissi]]'' (2024–25)
*{{flagicon|CMR}} [[Rigobert Song]] (2025–)
{{div col end}}

==Players==
===Current squad===
The following players were selected for the [[Exhibition match| friendly]] matches against [[Togo national football team|Togo]] and [[Angola national football team|Angola]]  in June 2026.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.facebook.com/photo/?fbid=1464282862377431&set=a.467581418714252|title=Liste des Fauves A publiée ce jour par le sélectionneur national Éloge ENZA.|publisher=facebook|date=20 May 2026|language=fr}}</ref>

''Caps and goals as of 9 June 2026, after the match against [[Angola national football team|Angola]].''<!-- ATTENTION! Substitutes for the June 2026 friendly against Angola are still unknown -->

{{nat fs g start}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Dominique Youfeigane]]|age={{Birth date and age|2000|2|7|df=y}}|caps=18|goals=0|club=[[S.K. Roeselare|Roeselare]]|clubnat=BEL}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Alladum Kolimba]]|age={{Birth date and age|1987|8|11|df=y}}|caps=3|goals=0|club=[[Remo Stars F.C.|Remo Stars]]|clubnat=NGA}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Eugene Thibault Ganazoui]]|age=|caps=0|goals=0|club=[[AS Douane|Douanes]]|clubnat=CTA}}
{{nat fs break}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Flory Yangao]]||age={{birth date and age|2002|1|13|df=y}}|caps=33|goals=0|club=[[Olympic Real de Bangui|Olympic Bangui]]|clubnat=CTA}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Hugo Gambor]]|age={{nowrap|{{birth date and age|2002|12|30|df=y}}}}|caps=7|goals=1|club=[[ES Troyes AC|Troyes]]|clubnat=FRA}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Sacha M'Baka]]|age={{birth date and age|2004|6|4|df=y}}|caps=3|goals=0|club=[[Stade Brestois 29|Brest B]]|clubnat=FRA}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Julien Kouadio]]|age={{birth date and age|1999|9|22|df=y}}|caps=2|goals=0|club=[[FC Annecy|Annecy]]|clubnat=FRA}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Maurice Selemby]]|age={{birth date and age|2004|1|31|df=y}}|caps=2|goals=0|club=[[GSD Castelfidardo Calcio|Castelfidardo]]|clubnat=ITA}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Wesley Zahibo]]|age={{birth date and age|2003|2|6|df=y}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=[[US Orléans|Orléans B]]|clubnat=FRA}}
{{nat fs g player|no=|pos=DF|name=[[Yoan Zouma]]|age={{birth date and age|1998|5|6|df=y}}|caps=1|goals=0|club=[[Lyon-La Duchère]]|clubnat=FRA}}
{{nat fs break}}

===Recent call-ups===
The following players have also been called up to the Central African Republic squad within the last twelve months.

{{nat fs r start}}
{{nat fs r player|no=|pos=GK|name=[[Désiré N'Zogué]]|age={{Birth date and age|1993|7|19|df=y}}|caps=2|goals=0|club=[[Yong Sports Academy|YOSA]]|clubnat=CMR|latest=Training camp March 2026}}

{{nat fs break}}
<sup>INJ</sup> Withdrew due to injury<br/>
<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary squad<br/>
<sup>RET</sup> Retired from the national team<br/>
<sup>SUS</sup> Serving suspension<br/>
<sup>WD</sup> Player withdrew from the squad due to non-injury issue.
{{nat fs end}}

==Records==
{{Updated|5 June 2026}}<ref>{{cite web |title=Central African Republic |url=https://www.national-football-teams.com/country/39/Central_African_Republic.html |website=National Football Teams}}</ref>
:''Players in '''bold''' are still active with Central African Republic.''

===Most appearances===
[[File:Foxi Kethevoama 2.jpg|200px|right|thumb|Foxi Kéthévoama is Central African Republic's most-capped player with 48 appearances.]]

|-

== Head-to-head record ==
''As of 9 June 2026 after match against [[Angola national football team|Angola]]''<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.eloratings.net/Cn_Africn_Rp.htm|title=World Football Elo Ratings: Central African Republic|publisher=Elo Ratings|access-date=16 July 2017|archive-date=18 July 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170718052605/http://www.eloratings.net/Cn_Africn_Rp.htm|url-status=live}}</ref>

== Honours ==
===Regional===
* '''[[CEMAC Cup]]'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (1):''' 2009
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (1): 2003
* '''[[UNIFAC Cup]]'''
** {{Silver2}} Runners-up (1): 1999

===Friendly===
